perturbation.original_text: 1. California Business and Professions Code Sections 20000 through 20043 provide rights to you concerning termination or non-renewal of a franchise. If the Master Franchise Agreement contains provisions that are inconsistent with the law, the law will control.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. OUR WEBSITE, www.originalsoupman.com, HAS NOT BEEN REVIEWED OR APPROVED BY THE CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF BUSINESS OVERSIGHT. ANY COMPLAINTS CONCERNING THE CONTENT OF THIS WEBSITE MAY BE DIRECTED TO THE CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF BUSINESS OVERSIGHT at www.dbo.ca.gov. 1. California Business and Professions Code Sections 20000 through 20043 provide rights to you concerning termination or non-renewal of a franchise. If the Master Franchise Agreement contains provisions that are inconsistent with the law, the law will control.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the disclosure about franchisee rights regarding termination/non-renewal of the franchise to the end of the addendum and placing it immediately after a website disclosure buries important information, making it less accessible to franchisees. This section should be at the beginning, to make it clear that it has priority, but it is not.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Sections 20000 through 20043
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Sections 20000 et seq. establishes the Franchise Relations Act, providing specific rights and protections to franchisees concerning termination and non-renewal of franchise agreements. By burying the disclosure, the franchisee is less likely to be aware of those rights, undermining the intent of the law to ensure franchisees are informed and protected. The location of the legal requirements has an impact on its clarity.

SPECIAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [18400 - 22949.92.2] ( Division 8 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 44. ) CHAPTER 5.5. Franchise Relations [20000 - 20044] ( Chapter 5.5 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) ARTICLE 1. Definitions [20000 - 20010] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) 20000. This chapter shall be known and may be referred to as the California Franchise Relations Act. (Added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. Effective October 1, 1980. Operative January 1, 1981, by Sec. 6 of Ch. 1355.) 20001. As used in this chapter, “franchise” means a contract or agreement, either expressed or implied, whether oral or written, between two or more persons by which: (a) A franchisee is granted the right to engage in the business of offering, selling or distributing goods or services under a marketing plan or system prescribed in substantial part by a franchisor; and (b) The operation of the franchisee’s business pursuant to that plan or system is substantially associated with the franchisor’s trademark, service mark, trade name, logotype, advertising, or other commercial symbol designating the franchisor or its affiliate; and (c) The franchisee is required to pay, directly or indirectly, a franchise fee. (d) “Franchise” does not include any of the following: (1) Any franchise governed by the Petroleum Marketing Practices Act (P.L. 95-297). (2) Lease departments, licenses, or concessions at or with a general merchandise retail establishment where the lease department, licensee, or concessionaire is incidental and ancillary to the general commercial operation of the retail establishment. Sales of a leased department, license, or concessionaire are incidental and ancillary to the general commercial operation of the retail establishment if they amount to less than 10 percent of the establishment’s sales. (3) A nonprofit organization operated on a cooperative basis by and for independent retailers which wholesales goods and services primarily to its member retailers and in which all of the following is applicable: (A) Control and ownership of each member is substantially equal. (B) Membership is l
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw by burying a disclosure about franchisee rights regarding termination/non-renewal, making it less accessible to franchisees. The original text prioritized this information, whereas the modified text places it after a website disclosure. This undermines the intent of California Business and Professions Code Sections 20000 through 20043, which aims to protect franchisees' rights concerning termination and non-renewal of franchise agreements, as described in the Law Explanation. The first scraped snippet, referencing California Franchise Relations Act in BPC Division 8, highlights the importance of this chapter. By obscuring this disclosure, the changed text diminishes the franchisee's awareness of their rights, contradicting the law's purpose.

perturbation.original_text: 6. The Master Franchise Agreement requires non-binding mediation. The mediation will occur in New York with the costs being borne by equally by the parties. Prospective franchisees are encouraged to consult private legal counsel to determine the applicability of California and federal laws (such as Business and Professions Code Section 20040.5 and Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281) to any provisions of a franchise agreement restricting venue to a forum outside the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Neither the franchisor, any person or franchise broker in Item 2 of the Disclosure Document is subject to any currently effective order of any national securities association or national securities exchange, as defined in the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, 15 U.S.C.A. 79a et seq., suspending or expelling such persons from membership in such association or exchange. 6. The Master Franchise Agreement requires non-binding mediation. The mediation will occur in New York with the costs being borne by equally by the parties. Prospective franchisees are encouraged to consult private legal counsel to determine the applicability of California and federal laws (such as Business and Professions Code Section 20040.5 and Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281) to any provisions of a franchise agreement restricting venue to a forum outside the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: California law may override venue restrictions forcing mediation/arbitration out of state. By placing the 'non-securities associations or exchange' disclosure immediately before the forum selection disclosure, it reduces the prominence and impact of the forum selection warning. It also creates topic discontinuity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.42
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.42 addresses the enforceability of forum selection clauses, particularly regarding healthcare contracts, and similar principles may apply to franchise agreements. By obscuring the warning, it makes it more difficult for a franchisee to understand their rights regarding venue and potentially leads them to believe the out-of-state forum is automatically enforceable. This contradicts the policy ensuring a fair venue for disputes.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.42. (a) The following provisions of a contract between the contractor and a subcontractor with principal offices in this state, for the construction of a public or private work of improvement in this state, shall be void and unenforceable: (1) A provision which purports to require any dispute between the parties to be litigated, arbitrated, or otherwise determined outside this state. (2) A provision which purports to preclude a party from commencing such a proceeding or obtaining a judgment or other resolution in this state or the courts of this state. (b) For purposes of this section, “construction” means any work or services performed on, or materials provided for, a work of improvement, as defined in Section 8050 of the Civil Code, and for which a lien may be claimed pursuant to Section 8400 of the Civil Code (whether or not a lien is in fact cl
perturbation.justification: The perturbation shifts the disclosure regarding venue restrictions, potentially diminishing its impact on the franchisee. The original text highlights the franchisee's right to consult legal counsel regarding the applicability of California and federal laws related to venue. By placing the disclosure after information regarding securities violations the placement diminishes the focus. This contradicts the policy of ensuring fair venue for disputes and protecting franchisees' rights as explained in the Law Explanation, as it obscures information relevant to those rights.

perturbation.original_text: 8. You must sign a general release if you renew or transfer your franchise. California Corporation Code 31512 voids a waiver of your rights under the Franchise Investment Law (California Corporations Code 31000 through 31516). Business and Professions Code 20010 voids a waiver of your rights under the Franchise Relations Act (Business and Professions Code 20000 through 20043).
perturbation.changed_text: 7. The Master Franchise Agreement requires application of the laws of New York. This provision may not be enforceable under California law. 8. You must sign a general release if you renew or transfer your franchise. California Corporation Code 31512 voids a waiver of your rights under the Franchise Investment Law (California Corporations Code 31000 through 31516). Business and Professions Code 20010 voids a waiver of your rights under the Franchise Relations Act (Business and Professions Code 20000 through 20043).
perturbation.explanation: The original text highlights that waivers of rights under the Franchise Investment Law and the Franchise Relations Act are void under California law, specifically when a franchisee renews or transfers their franchise and has to sign a release. By placing the New York law paragraph immediately before the paragraph about franchise waiver requirements, the disclosure is less clear, making it more difficult for parties to understand that rights waivers are voided.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code Section 31512 explicitly voids any waiver of rights granted to franchisees under the Franchise Investment Law. By positioning this information immediately after a statement about New York law potentially being unenforceable, the focus shifts away from the unwaivable nature of franchisee rights under California law, making it less prominent and potentially misleading. The juxtaposition suggests uncertainty where the law is definitive, hindering clear comprehension and potentially leading franchisees to unknowingly waive their rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CORP 31512 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Corporations Code - CORP TITLE 4. SECURITIES [25000 - 31528] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 1949, Ch. 384. ) DIVISION 5. FRANCHISE INVESTMENT LAW [31000 - 31528] ( Division 5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400. ) PART 6. GENERAL PROVISIONS [31510 - 31516] ( Part 6 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400. ) 31512. Any condition, stipulation or provision purporting to bind any person acquiring any franchise to waive compliance with any provision of this law or any rule or order hereunder is void. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1400.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a structural flaw by placing a paragraph about New York law before the disclosure about California law voiding waivers, potentially misleading franchisees. This violates the principle of clear disclosure as California Corporations Code Section 31512 states, "Any condition, stipulation or provision purporting to bind any person acquiring any franchise to waive compliance with any provision of this law or any rule or order hereunder is void." By obscuring this critical information, the changed text creates a contradiction, as it undermines the franchisee's ability to understand and assert their unwaivable rights under California law.
